  • 참조

  • 🇬🇧 English

Projection: SA, ED, CL methods

Source: vignettes/projection.Rmd
projection.Rmd

fit_ratio() projects cumulative loss ratio per cohort from a Triangle object. Three methods are available; this vignette explains the trade-offs.

Notation

For cohort ii at dev kk:

  • Ci,kLC^L_{i,k} — cumulative loss
  • Ci,kPC^P_{i,k} — cumulative risk premium (exposure)
  • fk=Ck+1L/CkLf_k = C^L_{k+1} / C^L_k — age-to-age (chain ladder) factor
  • gk=ΔCkL/CkPg_k = \Delta C^L_k / C^P_k — exposure-driven intensity
  • maturity point mgm_g — dev at which fkf_k stabilises for group gg (detected from CV / RSE thresholds)

Method 1: Stage-Adaptive ("sa", default)

The default method exploits the fact that fkf_k is volatile early and stable late, while gkg_k behaves the opposite way. SA switches estimators at the maturity point:

Ĉi,k+1L={Ĉi,kL+gk⋅Ci,kPk<mg(ED before maturity)fk⋅Ĉi,kLk≥mg(CL after maturity) \hat{C}^L_{i,k+1} \;=\; \begin{cases} \hat{C}^L_{i,k} + g_k \cdot C^P_{i,k} & k < m_g \quad \text{(ED before maturity)} \\ f_k \cdot \hat{C}^L_{i,k} & k \ge m_g \quad \text{(CL after maturity)} \end{cases}

Behaviour:

  • Before maturity: anchors the loss estimate to premium volume. Avoids the volatile-link explosion that classical CL suffers when early fkf_k are noisy.
  • After maturity: preserves the cohort’s own observed level. Avoids the “all cohorts converge to the average” behaviour that pure ED suffers in the tail.

When to use:

  • Long-tail products where development extends across many years.
  • Recent cohorts (immature data) mixed with older cohorts (matured).
  • Health insurance cohorts with structural pre-/post-maturity difference (e.g. waiting period transitions).
library(lossratio)
data(experience)
tri <- as_triangle(
  experience[coverage == "surgery"],
  groups   = "coverage",
  cohort   = "uy_m",
  calendar = "cy_m",
  loss     = "incr_loss",
  exposure = "incr_exposure"
)

ratio_sa <- fit_ratio(tri, method = "sa")        # default
plot(ratio_sa, metric = "ratio")

summary(ratio_sa)
#>     coverage     cohort     latest   loss_ult    reserve exposure_ult
#>       <char>     <Date>      <num>      <num>      <num>        <num>
#>  1:  surgery 2023-01-01  410248522  410248522          0    274192564
#>  2:  surgery 2023-02-01  976330445 1001441303   25110858    665667720
#>  3:  surgery 2023-03-01  978486045 1026151243   47665198    702047332
#>  4:  surgery 2023-04-01 2029909919 2186771221  156861302   1464399410
#>  5:  surgery 2023-05-01  624219436  697669301   73449865    483147255
#>  6:  surgery 2023-06-01  802880717  931393934  128513217    591568799

#> 26:     207578746   0.063347056 0.063167737 0.063347056   0.8733628   1.1209758

#> 30:     138920305   0.175966808 0.177412077 0.175966808   0.6604920   1.3559346
#> 31:     169134660   0.162558873 0.196729788 0.162558873   0.8246231   1.5957897
#> 32:     187372862   0.185820014 0.180653947 0.185820014   0.6181233   1.3262738
#> 33:     182485783   0.233059672 0.298541761 0.233059672   0.6958359   1.8660981
#> 34:     340964049   0.170378531 0.254743029 0.170378531   0.9958720   1.9944464
#> 35:     191871774   0.332559535 0.323480658 0.332559535   0.3386896   1.6067104
#> 36:     468598419   0.375910442 0.458260104 0.375910442   0.3208939   2.1172405
#>     loss_total_se loss_total_cv    ratio_se    ratio_cv ratio_ci_lo ratio_ci_hi
#>             <num>         <num>       <num>       <num>       <num>       <num>

Method 2: Exposure-Driven ("ed")

All future increments use ED:

Ĉi,k+1L=Ĉi,kL+gk⋅Ci,kP \hat{C}^L_{i,k+1} = \hat{C}^L_{i,k} + g_k \cdot C^P_{i,k}

Behaviour:

  • Stable when premium volume is informative across full development.
  • Loses the cohort-specific level signal — cohorts with higher observed loss converge toward the group-level gkg_k.

When to use:

  • Short-tail products where chain ladder offers no advantage.
  • Sparse data where age-to-age factors are unreliable across all links.
  • Comparing against SA / CL for sanity check.
ratio_ed <- fit_ratio(tri, method = "ed")
plot(ratio_ed, metric = "ratio")

Method 3: Classical Chain Ladder ("cl")

Classical Mack (1993) model:

Ĉi,k+1L=fk⋅Ĉi,kL \hat{C}^L_{i,k+1} = f_k \cdot \hat{C}^L_{i,k}

Behaviour:

  • Standard reserving practice. Equivalent to fit_cl(tri, loss = "loss") for the loss projection, but fit_ratio() additionally projects exposure forward via CL on exposure and computes the loss-ratio uncertainty via the delta method.
  • Volatile when early fkf_k are noisy — small denominators amplify link errors.

When to use:

  • Mature, stable portfolios where age-to-age factors are well-behaved across the full development.
  • Reserving exercises where regulators expect the classical Mack form for documentation.
ratio_cl <- fit_ratio(tri, method = "cl")
plot(ratio_cl, metric = "ratio")

Comparison 

ratios <- list(
  sa = fit_ratio(tri, method = "sa"),
  ed = fit_ratio(tri, method = "ed"),
  cl = fit_ratio(tri, method = "cl")
)

# Cohort-level summary
summary(ratios$sa)$loss_ult
#>  [1]  410248522 1001441303 1026151243 2186771221  697669301  931393934
#>  [7] 3050990158  488218204 1751869308 1311793843  848103123 1497869029
#> [13] 2901492851 1160045952  686574148 5687484014 2645801838 1209024555
#> [19] 2542927190  918120582  635470028  856446521  260916096  295637296
#> [25]  710560093 3276849152  434950057  356301148  697290587  789468799
#> [31] 1040451732 1008356733  783000257 2001214863  576954661 1246569307
summary(ratios$ed)$loss_ult
#>  [1]  410248522 1001304261 1027365215 2186835972  700124202  924502357
#>  [7] 3028986426  488454953 1725804921 1308019740  876716310 1527010394
#> [13] 2942802614 1193629493  685046660 5424401591 2740753232 1170293302
#> [19] 3461664518 1212435170  870725770 1217843289  398006955  456590846
#> [25] 1064623873 4386331021  727050149  616924302 1330756277 1072907077
#> [31] 1209357471 1432029264  865239645 1911124852  828091909 1442904476
summary(ratios$cl)$loss_ult
#>  [1]  410248522 1001441303 1026151243 2186771221  697669301  931393934
#>  [7] 3050990158  488218204 1751869308 1311793843  848103123 1497869029
#> [13] 2901492851 1160045952  686574148 5687484014 2645801838 1209024555
#> [19] 2542927190  918120582  635470028  856446521  260916096  295637296
#> [25]  710560093 3276849152  434950057  356301148  697290587  789468799
#> [31] 1040451732 1008356733  783000257 2001214863         NA         NA

Variance and confidence intervals

fit_ratio() reports analytical standard errors for L/P. Two variants:

  • se_method = "fixed" (default) — premium treated as fixed, SE(L/P)≈SE(L)/P\mathrm{SE}(L/P) \approx \mathrm{SE}(L)/P. Strictly the delta method with Var(P) = 0 and Cov(L,P) = 0.
  • se_method = "delta" — full delta method including premium uncertainty and loss-premium correlation rho:

Var(L/P)≈Var(L)P2+L2Var(P)P4−2ρLSE(L)SE(P)P3 \mathrm{Var}(L/P) \approx \frac{\mathrm{Var}(L)}{P^2} + \frac{L^2 \mathrm{Var}(P)}{P^4} - \frac{2 \rho L \mathrm{SE}(L) \mathrm{SE}(P)}{P^3}

Bootstrap intervals are also available:

ratio_boot <- fit_ratio(tri, method = "sa", bootstrap = TRUE, B = 1000, seed = 1)
summary(ratio_boot)
#>     coverage     cohort     latest   loss_ult    reserve exposure_ult
#>       <char>     <Date>      <num>      <num>      <num>        <num>
#>  1:  surgery 2023-01-01  410248522  410248522          0    274192564
#>  2:  surgery 2023-02-01  976330445 1001441303   25110858    665667720
#>  3:  surgery 2023-03-01  978486045 1026151243   47665198    702047332

#>            <num>     <num>         <num>        <num>         <num>
#>  1:    1.4962059 1.4962059             3            0             0

#>     loss_total_se loss_total_cv    ratio_se    ratio_cv ratio_ci_lo ratio_ci_hi
#>             <num>         <num>       <num>       <num>       <num>       <num>

Choosing a method

SA combines ED before the maturity point with CL after, so "sa" is the natural default. "cl" and "ed" are special cases that apply only when one of SA’s two regions becomes redundant.

Default is "sa" — ED before maturity, CL after.

Pick "cl" or "ed" only as special cases:
  ├── All cohorts are already past maturity
  │     → "cl"  (no ED region, so SA reduces to CL)
  └── Loss development is unstable across all dev and exposure is
      the more reliable signal
        → "ed"  (CL region is better served by exposure)

In practice: start with "sa" (the default), then run "cl" and "ed" for sensitivity. If all three agree, the projection is robust. If they diverge, inspect maturity detection and the underlying ATA factors.

Maturity input

For method = "sa" the maturity point mgm_g determines where the projection switches from ED to CL. By default fit_ratio() / fit_loss() use maturity = "auto", which calls detect_maturity() internally with default thresholds. Other forms:

  • maturity = NULL — disable detection (only meaningful for non-SA methods or for fit_ata() standalone).
  • maturity = maturity_spec(max_cv = 0.05, min_run = 2L) — lazy spec forwarding custom thresholds to detect_maturity() at fit time (leakage-safe for backtest()).
  • maturity = detect_maturity(tri, ...) — pre-built Maturity object, fixed across refits.
  • maturity = maturity_at(coverage = "surgery", change = 4) — manual per-group override (e.g. company-standard k*k^*).

On this page

Developed by Seokhoon Joo.
